title: Turmeric
subtitle: The golden root of kitchen and clinic
excerpt: Curcuma longa, the rhizome at the heart of South Asian cooking and traditional medicine. Western herbal practice draws on its inflammation-calming and hepatic actions; the evidence base has grown considerably in recent decades.
sourceNotes: Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India, Vol. I, Part I, Curcuma longa reference entry. the World Health Organisation Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Vol. 1, Rhizoma Curcumae Longae. the herbal reference on Curcumae longae rhizoma, evidence summary and safety review. Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, turmeric entry, spice and dye notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that supports liver function. Used in traditional western herbalism to describe herbs that aid the organ's digestive and clearing work.
The polyphenol pigments responsible for turmeric's yellow colour and most of its studied biological activity. Curcumin is the principal curcuminoid.

content.text: Turmeric is a rhizome: a horizontal underground stem that puts out roots below and shoots above. The plant (Curcuma longa, family Zingiberaceae) is a close relative of ginger and cardamom. It grows in warm, humid climates, and while India produces the great bulk of the world's supply, the plant has been cultivated across South and South-East Asia for at least four thousand years. Fresh turmeric looks like a stubby ginger root stained vivid orange; dried and ground, it becomes the powder that colours curries, rice dishes, and golden milk. The rhizome smells warm and slightly earthy, with a hint of pepper and a mild bitterness that lingers.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Anticoagulants. Curcumin has mild antiplatelet activity. Readers on warfarin, aspirin at therapeutic dose, clopidogrel, or other anticoagulants should keep turmeric to culinary quantities and consult their prescriber before any supplement or concentrated preparation.

Gallstones. Turmeric stimulates bile flow strongly. If stones obstruct the bile duct, this stimulation can cause a painful episode. Do not use concentrated turmeric preparations if you have diagnosed gallstones.

Liver caution at high dose. Standard culinary use is well tolerated. High-dose isolated-curcumin supplements (1 g+ curcumin daily) have generated isolated reports of liver harm in susceptible individuals. The food form at culinary doses does not carry this risk.

Paediatric use. Culinary use is appropriate for all ages. Medicinal-dose preparations are not for children without specialist herbalist advice.

content.text: Latin binomial: Curcuma longa. Family: Zingiberaceae. Parts used: the rhizome, dried and ground, or used fresh. The plant reaches 60-90 cm in height, with long strap-like leaves and occasional yellow flowers. Harvested 7-10 months after planting, once the leaves begin to yellow. The rhizome stains everything it touches, which is both its identity and a practical warning to wear gloves when preparing it fresh.
The primary active compounds are the

: curcumin (the principal compound), demethoxycurcumin, and bisdemethoxycurcumin. They make up roughly 2-5% of the dried root. Turmerone, an aromatic oil compound, contributes to the flavour and some traditional uses. Western herbalists draw on the inflammation-calming action (associated with curcumin's modulation of inflammatory pathways), the

action (traditional use for supporting liver and digestive function), and the antioxidant activity identified in modern research. Curcumin has poor bioavailability on its own; traditional preparations combine it with fat (ghee, coconut oil, whole milk) and black pepper (piperine increases absorption substantially), a combination the food tradition preserved long before the chemistry was understood.

Ayurveda records turmeric as a digestive, blood-purifying, and wound herb, used for skin, digestive, and respiratory complaints, liver, gallbladder, and recovery after childbirth. The Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (Vol. I, 1989) lists specific preparations and dosing guidance that has carried into modern practice.
Chinese herbal medicine (TCM) uses turmeric for moving qi and blood, relieving pain, and supporting the liver. TCM uses include period pain, chest and abdominal tightness, and liver complaints.

Western herbal practice adopted turmeric later than Asian traditions. Modern western herbalists use it for inflammation and sluggish digestion. The traditional use covers relief of mild digestive complaints.

The classic form is golden milk: ground turmeric simmered in whole milk with black pepper and fat, the preparation that best preserves absorption. Decoctions in water are used in TCM and Ayurveda. Adding black pepper and oil remains standard for improving absorption. Capsules of standardised curcumin extract are widely sold as supplements; the evidence for these is largely from this form, but the high-dose supplement form carries different safety considerations to the culinary use.

Anticoagulants: as above, keep to culinary quantities if on any blood-thinning medication. Gallstones: avoid concentrated preparations if stones are diagnosed. Pregnancy: culinary turmeric in food is standard and safe throughout pregnancy; the traditional herbal reference notes no safety concerns at culinary doses. Medicinal-dose preparations, concentrated extracts, or supplement capsules are outside the culinary safe-use context and should not be taken in pregnancy without specialist advice.
